figures.gloss: The Supreme Being, the nameless, formless source of all existence. It dwells in the highest unmanifest realms, emitting the ECK (the Light and Sound) to sustain creation.
figures.facet: Unlike personal creator gods who demand worship, the Sugmad is utterly abstract and oceanic; souls do not worship it directly but seek to realize their identity as drops of its infinite ocean.
figures.provenance: Coined by Paul Twitchell, adapting Radhasoami's 'Anami Purush' (the Nameless Lord).

figures.gloss: The Audible Life Stream or Holy Spirit, manifesting to the spiritual traveler as inner Light and Sound. It is the current that flows out from the Sugmad and eventually returns to it.
figures.facet: The ECK is strictly sensory in its inner manifestation; its sound—often described as a flute, rushing water, or buzzing—is the literal, navigable current the soul rides back to God.
figures.provenance: Derived directly from the Surat Shabd Yoga (Sound Current) of the Sant Mat and Radhasoami traditions.

figures.gloss: The highest state of God-consciousness on Earth, embodied in only one living teacher at a time. The Master acts as the 'Way-Shower,' guiding the chela outwardly through discourses and inwardly through the Dream Master.
figures.facet: The Living ECK Master is explicitly not to be worshipped as a god, but acts as a living compass and inner dream-guide, ensuring the soul is never alone while navigating the perilous lower planes.
figures.provenance: Central tenet established by Twitchell, echoing the guru-shishya dynamic of Radhasoami but democratized for a Western, New Age audience.

figures.gloss: A brotherhood of ascended spiritual masters—such as Rebazar Tarzs, Fubbi Quantz, and Yaubl Sacabi—who have supposedly guided humanity's spiritual evolution in secret for millennia.
figures.facet: They are presented as a hidden, immortal lineage living in the Himalayas or other dimensions, bridging Theosophical 'Ascended Masters' with the lineage of Sant Mat gurus, but entirely unmoored from historical indigenous origins.
figures.provenance: Codified by Twitchell (The Tiger's Fang, 1967); David C. Lane, The Making of a Spiritual Movement (1983), documents Twitchell lifting the Vairagi lineage concept and passages nearly verbatim from Julian Johnson's The Path of the Masters (1939) and from established Sant Mat/Radhasoami guru-lineages, rather than presenting an independently attested ancient tradition.

figures.gloss: The ruler of the lower worlds (Physical, Astral, Causal, Mental, and Etheric). Kal is the negative power, responsible for karma, reincarnation, time, and space, designed to temper and test the soul.
figures.facet: Kal is not intrinsically 'evil' like a Christian Satan, but a necessary cosmic administrator of illusion (maya) who fiercely guards his domain, trying to prevent souls from escaping into the pure spiritual worlds.
figures.provenance: A direct borrowing from the Sant Mat and Radhasoami figure of 'Kal' (Time/Death) or 'Brahm'.

figures.gloss: An ancient, sacred mantra sung aloud or internally to attune the practitioner's consciousness to the ECK. It is considered the most primal name of God and a love song to the creator.
figures.facet: It functions as a universal spiritual elevator; chanting it does not petition God for favors, but simply vibrates the soul at a higher frequency, lifting it out of the emotional and mental traps of the lower worlds.
figures.provenance: Appropriated from Sufi chanting (Dhikr) where 'Hu' means 'He' (God), repurposed by Twitchell as a universal sound current technique.

figures.gloss: The core technique of shifting one's awareness—not necessarily via out-of-body astral projection, but through expanding consciousness—to traverse the inner planes of existence and reach the Sugmad.
figures.facet: Eckankar explicitly differentiates Soul Travel from Astral Projection; the latter is seen as limited to the 'Lower Worlds' of illusion, while Soul Travel moves the true self beyond mind and matter entirely.
figures.provenance: Twitchell's foundational methodology, synthesizing Theosophical plane-travel with Sant Mat inner sound meditation.

figures.gloss: The American writer and former Church of Scientology staff member (his own birth year, given variously as 1908 or 1922, is disputed) who founded Eckankar in 1965, declaring himself the 971st Living ECK Master in an unbroken lineage of Vairagi adepts, and authored its foundational scripture and mythology.
figures.facet: The founder wrote himself into a lineage rather than claiming original revelation: Twitchell's synthesis of Sant Mat, Theosophy and popular occultism was presented as an ancient, secret transmission rather than a personal one — the opposite rhetorical move from most new-religious founders, who claim to originate rather than merely relay.
figures.provenance: Twitchell's own writings (The Tiger's Fang, 1967; Eckankar: The Key to Secret Worlds, 1969); David C. Lane, The Making of a Spiritual Movement: The Untold Story of Paul Twitchell and Eckankar (1983), on his Scientology background and undocumented biographical claims.

figures.gloss: The current Mahanta, Living ECK Master (spiritual name 'Wah Z'), who assumed the office in 1981 as Eckankar's third leader after Twitchell (1965-71) and Darwin Gross (1971-81); he relocated the organization to Chanhassen, Minnesota, built the Temple of ECK (dedicated 1990), and steered the movement's public presentation toward mainstream respectability.
figures.facet: Institutional continuity required smoothing over a contested handover: Klemp's succession followed disputes with Gross over rights to the 'Mahanta' title, and his long, stable tenure since 1981 has done more to normalize Eckankar's public face than any doctrinal development.
figures.provenance: Harold Klemp's own works (The Wind of Change, 1980, and later Eckankar organizational literature); David C. Lane's continuing documentation of Eckankar leadership succession.

figures.gloss: Eckankar's holy book, 'the Way of the Eternal,' published by Twitchell in two volumes (1970-71) and held by the tradition to be an earthly, partial transcript of a vastly longer scripture kept in its true form on the inner, non-physical planes.
figures.facet: The scripture announces its own incompleteness as doctrine: because the 'real' Shariyat is said to exist only on the inner planes, no earthly critique of the printed text's content or sourcing can be doctrinally decisive — the book is structured to be unfalsifiable by design.
figures.provenance: Paul Twitchell, The Shariyat-Ki-Sugmad, Books One and Two (1970-71); David C. Lane, The Making of a Spiritual Movement (1983), documenting extensive verbatim borrowing from Julian Johnson's The Path of the Masters (1939) and other Radhasoami-tradition sources.

figures.gloss: The purportedly 500-year-old Tibetan ECK Master of Eckankar's founding myth, said to have appeared to Paul Twitchell in 1965 and physically handed him the Rod of ECK Power, installing him as the 971st Living ECK Master and launching the movement.
figures.facet: The torchbearer who exists only in the telling: no independent record of Rebazar Tarzs predates Twitchell's own account, making him the clearest case in the roster of a legitimating figure invented whole to anchor a new teaching in an ancient-seeming lineage.
figures.provenance: Twitchell's foundational narrative (The Tiger's Fang, 1967); treated as a literary invention in David C. Lane, The Making of a Spiritual Movement (1983).

figures.gloss: Eckankar's charted plane-cosmology: Kal Niranjan's five lower worlds (Physical, Astral, Causal, Mental, Etheric) are surmounted by the pure spiritual God Worlds (the Soul Plane and above, ascending toward Anami Lok), each plane possessing its own characteristic Sound and Light by which the Soul Traveler navigates.
figures.facet: The cosmology is built as a route, not a residence: unlike heavens meant to be arrived at and inhabited, the God Worlds are primarily a sequence of way-stations, each identified by a distinct sensory signature that tells the traveling Soul which region it currently occupies.
figures.provenance: Paul Twitchell's plane-cosmology (The Shariyat-Ki-Sugmad; The Spiritual Notebook, 1971), adapting the Radhasoami/Sant Mat regional cosmology (cf. Julian Johnson, The Path of the Masters, 1939) to Eckankar's own nomenclature.

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: The ECK is the active, vibratory emanation of the utterly unmanifest Sugmad, acting as its voice in the lower realms.
Intra-tradition bond: Kal Niranjan rules the worlds of time, duality, and illusion, standing in direct contrast to the eternal, unified truth of the Sugmad.
Intra-tradition bond: The Living ECK Master is the current physical anchor and highest representative of the ancient Order of the Vairagi.
Intra-tradition bond: Singing the HU is the primary vibrational catalyst used to initiate and sustain the state of Soul Travel.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an utterly unknowable, formless ultimate source that emanates the universe rather than actively crafting it.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions posit an infinite, unmanifest absolute that can only be approached through its descending emanations.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Sugmad strongly parallels the Nirguna Brahman of Advaita Vedanta—an absolute, unqualifiable reality beyond all attributes.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are lower-world demiurges who believe they are the supreme god, trapping divine souls in material and mental illusion.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a structural 'negative power' that uses the illusions of mind, desire, and reincarnation to bind souls to the lower realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both serve as the lords of death, karma, and time, administering justice in the lower realms without being inherently 'evil'.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both rely on the deliberate detachment of the soul from the physical body to traverse unseen dimensions, though Eckankar aims for permanent spiritual liberation rather than immediate community intervention.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both envision the spiritual path as the deliberate extraction of divine light/soul from the heavy, imprisoning realms of matter and dark cosmic forces.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme embodiments of spiritual compassion who vow to remain active in the lower worlds to guide souls to liberation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are conceived as the impersonal, ever-flowing current that sustains the cosmos, which the practitioner must learn to align with and ride.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the fullness of divine light and sound that bridges the unmanifest ultimate and the lower, material realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both feature a lineage of enlightened pathmakers who have crossed the ocean of illusion and left a trail for others to follow.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are a specialized, immortal hierarchy of beneficent entities who assist the Supreme Being in maintaining cosmic order and guiding humanity.

PARENT SEAM: Paul Twitchell was an initiate of Kirpal Singh's Ruhani Satsang and reworked surat shabd yoga into the ECK and Soul Travel — borrowings (including from Julian Johnson's The Path of the Masters) documented in David Lane, The Making of a Spiritual Movement (1983).
PARENT SEAM: Eckankar's ruler of the lower worlds carries the Radhasoami figure over with the name itself intact — 'Kal' — one of the clearest documented borrowings (Lane 1983).
PARENT SEAM: the Living ECK Master is the Radhasoami living-satguru office transplanted to an American audience — one living master at a time, initiation and inner guidance included (Lane 1983).
Twitchell established the office of Mahanta by declaring himself its first modern holder in 1965.
Twitchell is the author who set down Eckankar's scripture.
Rebazar Tarzs is the legendary figure who, in the founding myth, hands Twitchell the Rod of ECK Power in 1965.
Klemp is the current living holder of the office the Mahanta entry defines.
Klemp is the third Mahanta in the organizational succession Twitchell founded (following Darwin Gross, 1971-81, who is not separately rostered here).
Rebazar Tarzs is named within the Order of the Vairagi as one of its senior members.
Kal Niranjan's five lower worlds are the base tier of the same cosmology the God Worlds surmount.
The God Worlds are the charted map Soul Travel is the technique for navigating.
The topmost God World (Anami Lok) is the cosmology's closest approach to the unmanifest Sugmad itself.

PARENT SEAM: per David Lane (1983), substantial passages of the Shariyat-Ki-Sugmad are lifted near-verbatim from Julian Johnson's The Path of the Masters (1939), the Sant Mat/Radhasoami classic this corpus's own Surat Shabd Yoga entry cites as its source.

PARENT SEAM: Eckankar's ladder of lower worlds surmounted by the pure God Worlds reproduces the Sant Mat/Radhasoami vertical cosmology — Kal's territory topped by the silent, unmanifest Anami region — the same map transplanted (Lane 1983).

PARENT SEAM: Kirpal Singh directly initiated and taught Paul Twitchell in Ruhani Satsang before Twitchell founded Eckankar in 1965 — the documented discipleship (Lane 1983) underlying the corpus's other sant-mat/eckankar seams.

David C. Lane's critical scholarship documents Paul Twitchell recasting the Radhasoami Anami Purush — the Nameless Lord above all regions — as Eckankar's Sugmad, renamed and re-mythologized for an American audience.

dossier.essence: A new religious movement founded in 1965 by the American writer Paul Twitchell, teaching 'Soul Travel' — the movement of awareness through inner planes carried by the 'Light and Sound of God' (the ECK). It is led by a single 'Living ECK Master' (the Mahanta) at a time — Harold Klemp since 1981 — and administered from the Temple of ECK in Chanhassen, Minnesota, with members mainly in North America and a scattered international following.
dossier.cosmos: Reality is a ladder of planes: the lower worlds of matter, emotion, and mind — governed by Kal Niranjan, the 'negative power' who administers karma, time, and reincarnation — rise through a pure spiritual region to the Sugmad, the formless, oceanic Supreme Being. Each soul is an eternal, individual spark of the Sugmad, wandering the lower worlds through many lifetimes until, guided by the Living ECK Master and the inner Light and Sound, it attains Self- and God-Realization and freedom from the wheel of rebirth. The teaching descends, in substance, from the Sant Mat / Radhasoami tradition of Surat Shabd Yoga (the 'yoga of the sound current').
dossier.practice: Members ('chelas') perform daily 'spiritual exercises,' above all singing HU — treated as an ancient sacred name of God — and inner contemplation to open the Light and Sound; they study monthly discourses, keep dream journals, and pass through a graded series of initiations. The ethic centers on personal spiritual unfoldment, detachment (vairag), loving service, and taking responsibility for one's own karma rather than petitioning the divine for favors.
dossier.canon: Paul Twitchell's writings — 'ECKANKAR: The Key to Secret Worlds' (1969) and 'The Tiger's Fang' among many — together with the movement's scripture, the 'Shariyat-Ki-Sugmad,' and the ongoing discourses of later Masters, chiefly Harold Klemp.
dossier.reflects: The impulse to convert mystical ascent into a teachable, repeatable technique — inner experience of the divine offered as a step-by-step method with a living guide, rather than a grace reserved for the rare saint.
sources: David C. Lane, 'The Making of a Spiritual Movement: The Untold Story of Paul Twitchell and Eckankar' (1983)
J. Gordon Melton, 'Encyclopedic Handbook of Cults in America' (rev. ed. 1992)
James R. Lewis, 'The Encyclopedia of Cults, Sects, and New Religions' (1998)
